title: Only God Is Good
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 11-3-24 -AM- Only God is Good - Luke 18_18-27 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Goodness as the Fruit of the Spirit
subpoints: Continuing series on the Fruit of the Spirit (Gal. 5:22-23)
All nine fruits are attributes of God that flow to believers
Goodness is the gathering of all other fruits together

outline.title: What Is Goodness? Starting With God
subpoints: Goodness is at the very essence of God's being (William Ames)
Goodness is God's love, mercy, long-suffering, and kindness put together
Goodness is the absence of any imperfection and the presence of perfect wholeness
Only God is good in this ultimate sense — we cannot know goodness without knowing God
The Psalms declare God's goodness repeatedly (Ps. 119:68; 25:8; 34:8; 86:5; 145:9)

outline.title: Goodness in Creation and Providence
subpoints: God called each day of creation "good" — reflecting who He is
Sin disrupted this goodness; God continues to deal with creation according to His goodness
Providence: God did not abandon creation to chance; He governs it according to His holy will
Matthew 7:11 — even imperfect fathers give good gifts; how much more God

outline.title: Goodness Questioned: The Problem of Evil
subpoints: How can God be good and the world be the way it is?
The question was first asked by Satan in the garden
Answer: God permits evil but has already at great personal cost overcome it through Christ
"All things work together for good" is grounded in the cross and resurrection

outline.title: Goodness in Christ
subpoints: In Luke 18:18-19, Jesus challenges the rich ruler: "Why do you call me good? No one is good but God."
Jesus was not denying His own goodness but forcing the man to reckon with His divine identity
Jesus is good because He obeyed and fulfilled God's good law (Rom. 7:12)
The two great commands: love God and love others — Jesus fulfilled them to the limit of death
The resurrection completes the picture of true goodness; Christ is the answer to evil's question

outline.title: Goodness in Us
subpoints: Romans 3:10-12: no one is naturally good
The new birth changes our nature and gives us capacity for goodness
The Holy Spirit produces the fruit of goodness in those united to Christ
We grow in goodness as we walk in the Spirit
Philip's invitation to Nathaniel: "Come and see" — the same invitation applies today

summary: In this Sunday morning sermon, Pastor Tito Lyro continues the series on the Fruit of the Spirit by examining "goodness" as listed in Galatians 5:22. He establishes that because all nine fruits are attributes of God, understanding goodness requires beginning with God Himself. Goodness is the very essence of God's being — His love, mercy, long-suffering, and kindness all put together — and the Psalms declare this goodness repeatedly. The sermon traces goodness through creation (where God called all things good), through providence (where God continues to govern the world for good), and through the problem of evil (which is answered only by the cross and resurrection of Jesus Christ). Turning to Luke 18:18-27, Pastor Lyro explains that Jesus' question to the rich ruler — "Why do you call me good?" — was not a denial of His own goodness but a challenge to the man to recognize Jesus as both God and man. Christ demonstrated ultimate goodness by obeying the law perfectly and dying for sinners. Finally, the sermon shows that through new birth and union with Christ, believers are transformed and empowered by the Spirit to bear the fruit of goodness in their relationships with one another.
transcript:
## The Fruit of the Spirit: Goodness

We are in the midst of the second half of our series on the Fruit of the Spirit, based on Galatians 5:22-23: "The fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace, long-suffering, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, self-control." This series has been both convicting and encouraging, as the Lord is working on hearts to produce all these fruits — and that is rooted in the gospel of Jesus Christ.

One challenge of this series is that the edges of these fruits are fuzzy. They overlap in meaning and interact with one another: love encompasses all the fruit; joy and peace always coexist; kindness and patience are twin virtues. Paul calls it the singular "fruit" of the Spirit, though he lists nine aspects. Today we come to goodness — a fruit that could be called the gathering of all the other fruits together.

## What Is Goodness? We Must Start With God

An attribute of God is something that describes and characterizes God. Goodness is one of those attributes. William Ames, a Dutch theologian from the 1700s, says that goodness is at the very essence of God's being. To know what goodness is, you must know who God is. Stephen Charnock, who wrote two volumes on the attributes of God, says: "This goodness is the true and genuine character of God. He is good. He is goodness. Good in himself. Good in his essence. Good in the highest degree."

But goodness defined more precisely: goodness is God's love, mercy, long-suffering, and kindness all put together and manifested toward Himself and toward others. God's goodness is the absence of any imperfection or defect and the presence of perfect wholeness. Only God is good in that ultimate sense.

Look at what the Psalms say: "You are good and do good" (Ps. 119:68). "Good and upright is the Lord" (Ps. 25:8). "O taste and see that the Lord is good" (Ps. 34:8). "For you, Lord, are good and ready to forgive, and abundant in mercy to all those who call upon you" (Ps. 86:5). "The Lord is good to all, and His tender mercies are over all of His works" (Ps. 145:9).

## Goodness in Creation and Providence

We can see the goodness of God in creation. As God creates the universe, with each act He looks at what He made and calls it good. Creation is good not merely because God liked what He did, or because it is useful or aesthetically pleasing — it is good because it reflects the One who created it, God Himself.

But sin terribly disrupts this goodness. Not long after God calls everything good, Genesis 6:5 records: "God saw that the wickedness of man was great in the earth, and that every intent of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually."

Yet even after sin, God continues to deal with creation according to His goodness through His acts of providence. The Belgic Confession, Article 13, says: "We believe this good God, after He created all things, did not abandon them to chance or fortune, but leads and governs them according to His holy will." Jesus Himself explained this in Matthew 7:11: "You, fathers who are not good people, give good gifts to your children — how much more will a good God give us good gifts in the way that He deals with us in everyday life." Brothers and sisters, the breath you just took is a gift from a good God.

## Goodness Questioned: The Problem of Evil

Many people call God's goodness into question: "If God is good, how can He allow suffering? How can He allow cancer? How can He allow my loved ones to go to hell?" This question is not new — it was first asked in a garden, before the Fall, by Satan himself: "Did God really say?" Satan was casting doubt on the goodness of God.

There is only one answer: look to Jesus Christ. To really understand God's goodness, we must look not only to creation and providence but also to redemption. A theologian puts it this way: "Evil exists, and God permits evil to exist, but God only actively permits evils that He has already at great personal cost determined to overcome for His great glory and our ultimate good." That personal cost was the death of His only Son. It is because of Jesus Christ that we can know with certainty that all things work together for good to those who love God.

## Goodness in Christ

If we return to Luke 18:18-19, we read of a certain ruler who asked Jesus: "Good teacher, what shall I do to inherit eternal life?" And Jesus replied: "Why do you call me good? No one is good but one, that is God."

At first it may seem that Jesus is denying His own goodness. But Jesus is actually challenging the young man's assumptions. He is saying: only God is good — and if you are not coming to me as the One who is both man and God, you have no business calling me good. Jesus is forcing the man to reflect on who He really is. As Thomas Brooks wrote: "Christ is the greatest good, the choicest good, the chiefest good, the most suitable good, the most necessary good. He is a pure good, a real good, a total good, an eternal good, and a soul-satisfying good."

Jesus is good because He obeyed, submitted to, and fulfilled God's law, which is by nature good (Ps. 119:39; Rom. 7:12). When asked the greatest commandment, Jesus summarized the law as loving the Lord your God with all your heart, soul, mind, and strength, and loving your neighbor as yourself (Mark 12:28-31). Jesus fulfilled both great commandments — even to the limit of death. Do you know what Jesus's limit was for His goodness? Death. God demonstrates His own love toward us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us.

The good shepherd not only lays down His life but takes it back up again. The resurrection completes the good news of the gospel. Look at the cross. But don't stop there — wait three days and see the resurrected Christ. Look to the reigning Christ, seated at the throne of David, ruling over His church. When we take all of that into account, the questions and doubts about God's goodness begin to have answers.

## Goodness in Us

Romans 3:10-12 is clear: "There is none good, no not one." How then can we speak of goodness in us? The new birth, followed by faith in Christ, changes our nature. When we put our faith in Christ, He takes us out of this broken and fallen world and places us in the realm of God's goodness. Our nature — the core of who we are — changes, and knowing Jesus and being united to Him transforms us and gives us the capacity for goodness.

As Psalm 25:8 says: "Good and upright is the Lord; therefore He teaches sinners in the way." Through Jesus Christ, God purposes to make us good and righteous as well. The new birth does not make us perfectly good — that is why we need to continually walk in the Spirit so that the fruit of goodness is cultivated in our hearts.

Remember Philip and Nathaniel? When Philip first met Jesus, he was so excited that he ran to tell his friend. But Nathaniel said: "Can anything good come out of Nazareth?" Philip's answer: "Come and see." Brothers and sisters, you have seen Jesus. Let us go and see Him again. And friend, if you have never seen Jesus — just try it. Come and see. He will not disappoint, because He is goodness in the flesh.
